title: The practice of taking up space without apologizing for existing
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
I spent years apologizing for my own existence 

 It started with small things. The way I'd shrink my body on public transit so the person next to me had...
htmlContent:
I spent years apologizing for my own existence

It started with small things. The way I'd shrink my body on public transit so the person next to me had more room. The way I'd preface every request with "I'm sorry, but..." The way I'd physically step back from conversations, making myself smaller, quieter, less visible. I didn't realize I was doing it. It felt like politeness. It felt like survival.

Here's the thing. That shrinking wasn't politeness. It was a trauma response wired deep into my nervous system. And I'm guessing if you're reading this, you know exactly what I'm talking about. You've felt that invisible force that compresses your shoulders, softens your voice, and makes you disappear into the background. You've apologized for taking up space before you even knew you were doing it.

This isn't about being rude or demanding. It's about the fundamental right to exist without constantly justifying your presence. Think about it. When was the last time you said "sorry" for something that wasn't your fault? When was the last time you made yourself physically smaller in a room full of people? When was the last time you held your breath, hoping no one would notice you?

I did this for decades. I learned it young. I learned that safety meant being invisible. That taking up space meant risking punishment. That my needs were an inconvenience. That my voice was too loud, my body too big, my opinions too much. I learned to apologize for everything. For breathing. For wanting. For being.

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, would call this a dorsal vagal response. It's the oldest part of our nervous system. The part that says freeze, collapse, disappear. When we can't fight or flee, we shut down. We make ourselves small. We become invisible to survive. And here's the cruel part: that survival strategy doesn't turn off when the danger passes. It becomes a default setting. A way of being in the world.

The apology reflex is a trauma symptom, not a virtue

I remember sitting in a coffee shop years ago. I was writing. A man walked past my table and his bag brushed my shoulder. I said "sorry." He didn't even notice. I apologized for being bumped into. That's how automatic it was. That's how deeply programmed this response can get.

I want to talk about something that doesn't get said enough in recovery spaces. Healing can feel like loss. When you start to let go of your survival strategies - the hypervigilance, the people-pleasing, the emotional walls - it can feel like you're losing yourself. Because those strategies were you, for a very long time. They were how you knew yourself.

Gabor Mate writes about this beautifully. The true self that got buried under survival adaptations - it's still there. But meeting it can be disorienting. Who am I without the anxiety? Who am I without the need to control everything? Who am I when I'm not performing for someone else's approval? These are terrifying questions. And they don't have quick answers.

But here's what I've found. On the other side of that disorientation is something real. Something that doesn't need to perform or protect or prove. Something that can just be. It's quiet. It's simple. And it's been waiting for you this whole time. You don't have to find it all at once. You just have to be willing to keep looking. Even when it's scary. Especially when it's scary.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body. It's not just a story we tell. It's a physical imprint. A pattern of tension, collapse, and guardedness that shapes how we move through space. When we apologize for existing, we're not just being polite. We're expressing a body that learned that safety comes from submission. From being small. From not taking up space.

I want you to try something right now. Just for a moment. Notice your body. Are you holding your breath? Are your shoulders hunched? Is your jaw tight? Are you making yourself smaller than you actually are? Sit with that. Don't judge it. Just notice it. That's your nervous system doing what it learned to do to keep you safe.

Wild, right? The body doesn't forget. It remembers everything. Every time you were told to be quiet. Every time your needs were dismissed. Every time you were punished for taking up space. The body keeps the score, as Bessel van der Kolk wrote. And that score shows up in the way we move, the way we speak, the way we apologize for existing.

I'm not saying this to make you feel broken. I'm saying this because naming the pattern is the first step toward changing it. And change is possible. It's slow. It's uncomfortable. But it's possible.

Taking up space is a practice, not a destination

There's no magic moment where you suddenly feel entitled to exist. It doesn't work that way. What works is practice. Small, daily, uncomfortable practice. The kind that makes your nervous system scream at first. The kind that feels wrong, selfish, dangerous. That's how you know it's working.

Start with your body. Sit in a chair and take up the full space. Don't cross your legs. Don't fold your arms. Let your shoulders fall back. Breathe. Notice how uncomfortable that feels. Notice the urge to shrink. Stay with it. Breathe again. That's the practice.

Start with your voice. Say what you need without apologizing first. "I need a glass of water." Not "I'm sorry, but could I possibly get a glass of water if it's not too much trouble?" Just the need. Just the request. Let the silence after feel uncomfortable. Let the urge to fill it with apologies pass. Breathe.

Start with your boundaries. This is where a lot of us get stuck. We've been trained to believe that boundaries are selfish. That saying no is rude. That protecting our energy is unkind. None of that is true. A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link). It walks through exactly how to set limits without feeling like a monster. Because that feeling of being a monster? That's just your nervous system panicking at the unfamiliar sensation of safety.

Here's the thing about this practice. It will trigger you. It will bring up old pain. It will make you want to run back to the safety of invisibility. That's normal. That's the work. Your nervous system is learning a new way to be in the world. And that takes time.

The nervous system needs to feel safe to take up space

You can't think your way out of this. I tried. I read every self-help book. I journaled. I meditated. I understood intellectually that I had a right to exist. But my body didn't believe it. My nervous system didn't trust it. The words were right, but the body was still bracing for punishment.

Let me be honest with you about something. Recovery is expensive. Not just financially - though therapy and bodywork and supplements and books add up fast. It's expensive in time, in energy, in relationships. Some people won't understand why you're changing. Some people benefited from your old patterns and they won't like the new ones. That's real. And it's painful.

But here's what's also real. The cost of not healing is higher. It's paid in health - in autoimmune conditions, in chronic pain, in shortened lifespan. It's paid in relationships - in repeating the same patterns, in choosing the same unavailable partners, in pushing away the people who could actually love you. It's paid in aliveness - in years spent numb, disconnected, going through the motions.

I'm not saying this to scare you. I'm saying it because the choice to heal isn't a luxury. It's a necessity. And it's one that only you can make. No one can do this work for you. But you don't have to do it alone. That's the paradox of recovery - it's deeply personal work that requires other people. You need witnesses. You need co-regulators. You need at least one person who can see you clearly and not look away. Find that person. Or let them find you.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Oprah Winfrey and Dr. Bruce Perry. It shifts the question from "what's wrong with you" to "what happened to you." And that shift matters. Because nothing is wrong with you for wanting to be invisible. Something happened to you that made invisibility feel like the safest option.

Your nervous system needs to feel safe before it can relax into taking up space. And safety isn't something you can logic your way into. It's something you have to feel. In your body. In your bones. In the slow, steady rhythm of your breath.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman. She writes about how recovery happens in stages. First, safety. Then, remembrance and mourning. Then, reconnection. You can't skip to reconnection without establishing safety first. And safety includes the right to take up space. The right to exist without apology.

So how do you help your nervous system feel safe? Slowly. Gently. By noticing when you're shrinking and choosing not to. By breathing when your body wants to hold its breath. By staying present when your mind wants to dissociate. By practicing, over and over, the simple act of existing without apology.

Shame is the barrier, not your needs

Underneath the apology reflex is shame. Deep, old shame. The belief that you are flawed. That your needs are too much. That your presence is a burden. That you have to earn the right to exist by being good, quiet, helpful, invisible.

That shame isn't yours. It was given to you. By caregivers who couldn't meet your needs. By a culture that profits from your smallness. By systems that punish you for taking up space. By trauma that taught you that visibility is dangerous.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. She talks about how we can learn to accept ourselves exactly as we are. Not as we think we should be. Not as we've been told we need to be. But as we actually are. Right now. In this moment. With all our mess and need and desire to be seen.

Radical acceptance isn't about giving up. It's about stopping the war with yourself. It's about recognizing that you don't have to earn your place in the world. You already have it. You always did. The only thing standing between you and that truth is the shame that says otherwise.

I'm not going to tell you that this is easy. It's not. I still catch myself apologizing for existing. I still feel my shoulders tighten when I walk into a room. I still hear that old voice that says "make yourself small, make yourself quiet, don't be a problem." But I also know now that it's a voice. Not a truth. A conditioned response. Not who I am.

And I know that I can choose differently. Not perfectly. Not all the time. But more often than I used to. And that's enough. That's the practice. Showing up, again and again, and choosing to take up space. Choosing to exist without apology. Choosing to be seen, even when it terrifies you.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link)
  • What Happened to You? (paid link)
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link)
